segments.title: What makes a multi-train railway journey reliable
segments.summary: A disrupted GWR journey provides a direct comparison with a smooth 14-hour, six-train trip across Japan and separates frontline service from system reliability.
segments.body: At Newton Abbot, Dr. Clarke contrasts disruption on his journey home from London Paddington with a much longer rail journey between Sasebo and Hakodate. The Japanese trip took about fourteen hours and six trains, yet its connections worked in sequence. On Great Western Railway, cancellations attributed to missing train managers, drivers, spare parts, and the effects of an earlier accident moved him through more trains before he was halfway home. He explicitly separates those operational failures from the conduct of station staff, whom he describes as professional, kind, and helpful. The comparison shows how staffing, maintenance, incident recovery, and timetable coordination combine to determine whether an interconnected railway feels reliable.

evidence.note: Clarke contrasts a smooth fourteen-hour, six-train journey in Japan with GWR cancellations attributed to staffing, spare parts, and an earlier accident, while praising station staff.
